d: To spend time relaxing with someone
To understand or find the solution to something
e: "I finally figured out how to fix it."

d: To use all of something so none is left
To mention a topic in conversation
e: "Don't bring up the argument at dinner."

d: It's okay; don't stress about it (informal reassurance)
To reach the same level as others; to update someone on news
e: "We need to catch up — it's been months!"

d: Somewhat; to a degree (softens what you're saying)
A polite way to introduce a request or question
e: "I was wondering if you could help me."

d: Proceed; you have permission to do it
To finally be in a situation you didn't plan for
To result in a particular way (often surprising)
To try something even if you're not sure about it
e: "You should give it a shot — what do you have to lose?"

d: Busy and moving around; constantly active
e: "I've been on the go all day."

d: To be logical or understandable
To handle or manage a problem or situation
To feel excited about something coming up
e: "I'm really looking forward to the vacation."

d: You are welcome/allowed to do something
Used to introduce a new, slightly off-topic point
e: "By the way, did you hear the news?"

d: As quickly as one can; abbreviated ASAP
To relax; also used as a casual goodbye
e: "Take it easy this weekend — you've been working too hard."

d: Used to urge someone; to express disbelief
e: "Come on, we're going to be late!"

d: To successfully do something difficult
To stay in contact with someone
e: "Let's keep in touch after you move."

d: To feel comfortable in a group; to belong socially
e: "It took time, but he finally fit in."

d: To become familiar with something unfamiliar
e: "I'm still getting used to the cold weather."

d: A casual greeting meaning "how are you / what's happening?"
To get something quick to eat
e: "Want to grab a bite after work?"

d: To stop working for the day
e: "I'm exhausted — let's call it a day."

d: To leave; to start a journey
e: "It's getting late, we should hit the road."

d: To relax and calm down
e: "Chill out, everything's going to be fine."

d: To wait patiently
e: "Hang tight, I'll be there in five minutes."

d: A casual way to admit a mistake ("my fault")
e: "My bad, I didn't see your message."

d: Seriously; truly (also asks "really?")
Certainly; you're welcome (enthusiastic yes)
Used to accept that a point is reasonable
Not important; nothing to worry about
e: "It's no big deal, I can reschedule."

d: To ease initial social awkwardness
e: "He told a joke to break the ice."

d: Something very easy
e: "The test was a piece of cake."

d: Contact me (call or message)
Used to summarize quickly
e: "Long story short, we missed the flight."

w: off the top of my head
d: From memory, without checking or thinking hard
e: "Off the top of my head, I'd say about twenty."

d: Approximately; plus or minus a little
e: "It's an hour away, give or take."

d: To be behind schedule
An advance warning or notice
e: "Thanks for the heads-up about the meeting."

d: Suddenly and unexpectedly
e: "She called me out of the blue after years."

d: Occasionally; not often
e: "We go out for dinner once in a while."

d: Said before something possibly hurtful, to soften it
e: "No offense, but that color isn't you."

d: A casual way to agree to a plan
You decide; it's your choice
Keep me updated with new information
e: "Keep me posted on how it goes."

d: "Laughing out loud" — shows something is funny
"Be right back" — stepping away briefly
"By the way" — introduces an aside
e: "btw, are we still on for Friday?"

d: "I don't know"
"In my opinion" (imho = in my humble opinion)
"To be honest"
"For your information"
"Shaking my head" — shows disappointment or disbelief
"Oh my God" — surprise or excitement
"Talk to you later"
"No problem" — a casual "you're welcome"
"Direct message"; also used as a verb
"Too long; didn't read" — a short summary
"In real life" — offline, not online
"Fear of missing out"
e: "I have serious fomo about that trip."

d: "What are you doing?"
"I know, right?" — strong agreement
"Never mind"
Short for "thanks"
"Just kidding"
"Working from home"
Slightly; secretly; in a subtle way
Openly; very much (opposite of lowkey)
No lie; for real (cap = a lie)
e: "That was the best show ever, no cap."

d: Okay / sounds good / it's a deal
Suspicious or questionable
A mood or atmosphere; to get along well
To show off, especially wealth or success
To suddenly cut off all contact with someone
e: "We had two dates and then he ghosted me."

d: Bitter or upset, often over something small
e: "He's still salty about losing the game."

d: To do something exceptionally well
Embarrassing or awkward to witness
Mediocre; average at best
e: "The sequel was honestly kind of mid."

d: A big positive transformation in looks or life
e: "She had a serious glow up this year."

d: To share gossip or juicy news
To subtly insult or criticize someone
e: "She threw some shade at her rival."

d: A win or success (an "L" is a loss)
Stylish clothing; impressive fashion
Extremely good; impressive (very common in casual speech)
Extremely tired
Very hungry (exaggeration)
Having little or no money
e: "I can't go out, I'm broke this week."

d: Extremely busy; overwhelmed with work
Suspicious, unsafe, or unreliable
e: "That part of town feels a bit sketchy at night."

d: Genuine, legitimate; also "really"
e: "Is this deal legit?" / "The food was legit amazing."

d: Causing social discomfort or embarrassment
Very excited and energized
Disappointed or sad
Annoyed and out of patience with something
e: "I'm fed up with the constant delays."

d: Unexpected, strange, or without clear reason (casual)
e: "That was such a random thing to say."

d: Completely exhausted (also "wiped out")
Feeling unable to cope with too much
e: "I felt overwhelmed by all the emails."
